summary:
The Novgorod Republic (c. 1136–1478 AD) was the most distinctive political entity in medieval Russia — an oligarchic republic governed by an assembly of free citizens (the veche) and a council of wealthy merchants and landowners (the boyars), rather than the princely autocracy that characterized other Russian states. Located in northwestern Russia on the Volkhov River, Novgorod was a major commercial hub of the [[event:2300|Hanseatic League]] network, trading Russian furs, wax, and honey for western European cloth, metals, and wine, and maintaining trade relationships that made it one of the wealthiest cities in medieval Europe.

Novgorod's political system was unique in Russia: the veche could elect and dismiss princes (who served as military commanders but had strictly limited political authority), elect the archbishop (the spiritual and temporal head in practice), and make major decisions by popular assembly. This quasi-democratic tradition — however dominated in practice by the merchant oligarchy — stood in sharp contrast to the autocratic tradition developing in Moscow and generated a distinct Novgorodian civic culture and local patriotism. Novgorod's territory was enormous — the largest medieval state in Europe by area, extending from the Baltic to the Urals and into Siberia — and its fur trade wealth made it exceptionally powerful. The republic was forcibly absorbed by [[event:3033|Moscow]] under Ivan III in 1478 following a military campaign; Ivan's suppression of Novgorodian civic institutions and mass deportations of its leading families eliminated the republic's distinct identity, but the memory of Novgorodian democracy has endured as a symbol in Russian historical consciousness of an alternative path not taken.
